title: Heat pump flow temperature: why it matters and what to set it to
subtitle: The relationship between flow temperature, COP, and radiator sizing
excerpt: A heat pump running at 55°C flow temperature operates at a COP of around 2.0-2.5. The same heat pump at 35°C flow operates at COP 3.5-4.5. Reducing the flow temperature by 10°C improves efficiency by approximately 20-30%, but only if the heat emitters (radiators or underfloor heating) can deliver enough heat at the lower temperature.
sourceNotes: Heat Pump Association technical guides on flow temperature and efficiency. HHIC Best Practice guidance for heat pump system commissioning. Carbon Trust and Energy Saving Trust guidance on heat pump performance. Nibe and Vaillant installation manuals for flow temperature setting guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: The temperature of the water leaving the heat pump heading to the radiators or underfloor heating. Lower flow temperatures deliver higher efficiency (COP) but require larger heat emitters to deliver the same amount of heat to the room.

Coefficient of Performance. The ratio of heat output to electrical input: COP 3 means 1 kWh of electricity produces 3 kWh of heat. COP falls as flow temperature rises; it also falls as outdoor temperature drops. The SCOP (seasonal COP) is the annual average.

The temperature difference between the flow and return water in a heating circuit. Standard boiler design is Delta T 20°C (e.g. flow 70°C, return 50°C). Heat pump design target is Delta T 5-7°C (e.g. flow 40°C, return 33-35°C) because lower Delta T and lower flow temperature both improve COP.

A heat pump or boiler control mode that varies flow temperature based on outdoor temperature: higher flow temperature on cold days, lower flow temperature on mild days. Reduces electricity consumption on mild days and is standard practice for heat pump installations.

content.text: rises: this is a fundamental thermodynamic constraint, not a design flaw. A heat pump running at 45°C flow uses around 20% less electricity per unit of heat than the same pump running at 55°C. Running at 35°C saves around 35% versus 55°C. The UK electricity price of 27p/kWh versus gas at 6p/kWh means a heat pump needs a COP of 4.5 just to match a gas boiler on running cost. Achieving that requires flow temperatures below 40°C in typical UK conditions.
Why most UK heat pump installations run too hot

Heat pumps are often installed into existing houses with radiators sized for a gas boiler at 70°C flow temperature. A radiator sized for 70°C flow delivers its rated heat output. At 45°C flow, the same radiator delivers approximately 45% of its rated output. If the original radiator sizes were only just adequate for the room heat loss (a common situation in older installations), reducing flow temperature results in the rooms not reaching temperature on cold days, and the installer raises the flow temperature to compensate.

The correct solution is to size the radiators for the target flow temperature before or during installation, not to raise the flow temperature to suit undersized emitters. See the linked tutorial on radiator sizing for heat pump operation.

is the control mode that varies flow temperature with outdoor temperature. On a mild day (10°C outside) the heat loss from the house is low: the heating system only needs to supply a small amount of heat, and a low flow temperature (35-38°C) is sufficient. On the coldest design day (-3°C in most of England) the full heat pump output is needed and the flow temperature rises to its maximum (typically 45-50°C for an air-source heat pump). Most modern heat pumps have weather compensation built in; it just needs enabling and calibrating by the installer.

A 1930s semi in Leeds with a calculated design heat loss of 7 kW at -3°C. The heat pump is an 8 kW unit rated at COP 3.4 at 7°C outdoor, 35°C flow. With the flow temperature set to 55°C by the original installer, the measured running COP (from electricity and heat meters) is 2.1 over the heating season: 1 kWh electricity generates 2.1 kWh heat. Annual electricity for heating: approximately 4,200 kWh.

After replacing four radiators with larger models and enabling weather compensation (flow temperature range 38-48°C), the seasonal COP rises to 3.2. Annual electricity for heating: approximately 2,760 kWh, a saving of approximately 1,440 kWh/year.

in a heat pump circuit should be 5-7°C, compared with 20°C for a gas boiler. This means the pump needs to circulate water about three times faster (higher flow rate) to deliver the same heat output at a lower temperature difference. Existing pipework from a gas boiler system is usually large enough, but the circulating pump may need upgrading and the system must be balanced (flow rates adjusted at each radiator) to achieve an even distribution.
